snippets: Amberg developed into an important city during the medieval period.
Amberg became one of the most important cities in the Upper Palatinate.
Amberg served as a residence city of the Electors Palatine.
Amberg was a major center of iron ore trade in the Middle Ages.
Iron ore was mined within the city at the Erzberg.
Iron ore from Amberg was processed in hammer mills.
Hammer mills in the region were powered by water.
Processed iron was transported from Amberg using flat-bottomed boats.
Flat-bottomed boats carried iron downstream.
Iron from Amberg was transported to Regensburg.
In Regensburg, iron was exchanged for salt.
In Regensburg, iron was exchanged for grain.
Amberg had trade connections with Ulm.
Amberg had trade connections with Passau.
Amberg had trade connections with Hungary.
Amberg formed trade agreements with Nuremberg.
Amberg formed trade agreements with Sulzbach.
These agreements helped dominate the regional iron market.
The Thirty Years’ War had a severe impact on Amberg.
During the war, Amberg lost its dominance in the iron trade.
Other regions gained importance in iron production during this period.
Hammer mills became outdated during this time.
After 1620, Amberg became part of Bavaria.
The ruling dynasty after 1620 was the Wittelsbach family.
Amberg underwent forced re-Catholicization.
Many Protestant merchants left the city.
Protestant merchants relocated to Nuremberg.
Protestant merchants relocated to Regensburg.
The departure of merchants weakened the local economy.
The Upper Palatinate region became known as the poorhouse of Bavaria.
Industrial development in Amberg began around the 19th century.
Hirschauer kaolin sand was processed into ceramics.
Kaolin sand was referred to as white gold of the Upper Palatinate.
The Royal Bavarian Rifle Factory relocated to Amberg.
The relocation of the rifle factory brought new jobs.
The relocation of the rifle factory increased population growth.
Amberg was connected to the railway network in 1859.
The railway connection came later than in other Bavarian cities.
A blast furnace was built in Amberg in 1883.
The blast furnace later became known as the Luitpoldhütte.
The Emailfabrik Baumann was founded by a craftsman family.
The family originated from Upper Franconia.
The factory initially employed around 50 workers.
The factory produced enamelled tin goods.
Production used a glass-coating process called Glasfluss.
The company expanded to employ several thousand workers.
Many workers in the factory were women.
Two-thirds of production was exported internationally.
Exports reached South America.
Exports reached Africa.
Exports reached India.
The factory buildings dominated the city skyline.
Industrial growth caused rapid population increase in Amberg.
Siemens relocated operations to Amberg in 1948.
The relocation occurred after World War II.
The Siemens plant was moved from what became East Germany.
The Gerätewerk Amberg was established by Siemens.
The Siemens Elektronikwerk Amberg opened in 1989.
The factory focuses on electronics manufacturing.
The factory was named a Digital Lighthouse Factory in 2021.
The recognition was awarded by the World Economic Forum.
Approximately 4,500 employees work at the facility.
A new glassworks was built in Amberg in the late 1960s.
The glassworks was commissioned by the company Rosenthal.
The building was designed by architect Walter Gropius.
Walter Gropius was the founder of the Bauhaus movement.
The building is known as the Glass Cathedral.
The Glass Cathedral features a triangular gable structure.
The Glass Cathedral is an example of industrial architecture.
Grammer is an internationally successful manufacturer based in Amberg.
Grammer produces seating systems.
Grammer began producing seats for ICE trains in 1990.
World War I ended the industrial boom in Amberg.
The 1920s brought economic challenges to the city.
High tariffs affected local industries.
Unemployment increased during this period.
Strikes occurred in the workforce.
Several factories closed during the 1920s.
Forced laborers were used in Amberg during World War II.
Prisoners of war were used in industrial production.
The blast furnaces of the Luitpoldhütte were shut down in 1968.
The Emailfabrik Baumann declared insolvency in 1986.
Many businesses closed during structural economic change.
Amberg has a modern industrial economy.
Key sectors include metalworking.
Key sectors include mechanical engineering.
Key sectors include electronics.
Key sectors include the printing industry.
Key sectors include automation technology.
Key sectors include environmental technology.
Key sectors include energy technology.
Siemens is a major employer in Amberg.
Lüdecke is a major employer in Amberg.
Baumann GmbH is a major employer in Amberg.
Deprag is a major employer in Amberg.
Herding Filtertechnik is a major employer in Amberg.
Grammer is a major employer in Amberg.
The Siemens visitor center in Amberg is called THE IMPULSE.
THE IMPULSE opened in April 2021.
The building has an area of 5,400 square meters.
THE IMPULSE spans three floors.
The building includes a central atrium.
The roof spans 17 meters without supports.
The building uses sustainable energy systems.
The building includes rooftop photovoltaic panels.
The building uses heat recovery systems.
Electric vehicle charging stations are available.
The building uses LED lighting.
THE IMPULSE showcases digitalization technologies.
THE IMPULSE showcases automation technologies.
THE IMPULSE presents intelligent electrical infrastructure.
THE IMPULSE demonstrates autonomous manufacturing concepts.
Technologies include 5G connectivity.
Technologies include edge computing.
Technologies include cloud computing.
Technologies include artificial intelligence.
Technologies include blockchain.
The Open Space Lab is part of THE IMPULSE.
The Open Space Lab has an area of 325 square meters.
The Open Space Lab is used for collaboration.
Collaboration includes universities.
Collaboration includes startups.
Collaboration includes industry partners.
Access to THE IMPULSE is available through guided tours.
Virtual tours of THE IMPULSE are available.
